titleStmt.title: NHS, CDC, and WHO Twitter Health Communication: A Preliminary Shiny App

author.affiliation: University of Georgia, United States of America

keywords.term: I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon

body.p: This work reports on ongoing and preliminary development of an R Shiny application to display and understand trends in health communication by three key public health agencies during 2020. Specifically, the data used for this application are a corpus of tweets by official accounts of the National Health Service (NHS), the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), and the World Health Organization (WHO) throughout 2020. The dataset has been examined previously using different techniques through semantic analysis of trigrams and keywords (Ireland 2024) but has not been made available for users to interact with and analyze the texts. Figure 1 displays an example visualization of CDC trigrams for users to view.

At present, the application only includes English language tweets, although the full dataset includes other languages like Spanish and Hindi. Linguistic data and characteristics are gathered and compiled using R, specifically the following packages: tidytext (Silge and Robinson 2016), udpipe (Wijffels 2021), tidyverse (Wickham et al. 2019) and quanteda (Benoit et al. 2018). Visualizations are created using ggplot2 from the tidyverse (Wickham and Grolemund 2017, Wickham et al. 2019).

Related studies have considered the language of COVID-19 and of other health communication scenarios through news and social media, for example (Collins and Nerlich 2016, Gui et al. 2018, Hunt and Harvey 2015, Hunt and Brookes 2020, Semino and Koller 2020, Ireland 2024), but no studies have used R shiny to visualize textual analytics and differences in units like the three listed above. Additionally, the dataset provides a unique perspective on CDC, NHS, and WHO public health communication during the COVID-19 pandemic. Official accounts analyzed are available by request and are composed of over 2 million words.

This presentation will highlight the current version of the Shiny application and visualizations and discuss additional plans for development. This work will be of interest to interdisciplinary researchers because of the methodologies involved and because of the topic area of public and governmental health communication on social media.
